nativeRange.habitat: Shallow estuarine waters over sand and mud bottoms, often associated with seagrass beds; tolerates a wide salinity range from near-freshwater to fully marine.

decorPreferences: bare-bottom culture vessel preferred for easy cleaning
gentle aeration via airstone (no powerheads — mysids are easily damaged)
fine mesh screens over any overflow or drain to prevent loss
dim, indirect lighting on a 16h light / 8h dark cycle

diet.notes: In the wild they graze on zooplankton, phytoplankton and detritus. In culture they are generally fed newly hatched Artemia nauplii once or twice daily; live phytoplankton and very finely ground flake or pellet powder can supplement. Mysids are themselves voracious — adults will cannibalise juveniles if underfed.

goodWith: kept in a dedicated culture vessel separate from display livestock
harvested as live feed for seahorses, pipefish, mandarin dragonets, and the fry of pelagic-spawning marine fish
avoidWith: any fish or invertebrate in the same vessel — mysids are prey, not tankmates

summary: A tiny estuarine opossum shrimp cultured at home as a live food for picky reef species such as seahorses, pipefish and mandarin dragonets; an aquaculture project rather than a display animal.
careNotes:
Mysid Shrimp (Americamysis bahia) is not a true shrimp — it belongs to the order Mysida, a group known as opossum shrimps because the females carry developing young in a marsupium (a brood pouch similar to that of a marsupial). The older name Mysidopsis bahia, used before a 1999 reclassification, is still common in aquaculture and toxicology literature. The species is best known outside the hobby as the EPA's standard saltwater toxicity-test organism, which means its captive husbandry is unusually well documented for an invertebrate of its size.

In the aquarium world, Mysid Shrimp are kept almost exclusively as a CULTURE PROJECT — an in-home source of live feed for fish that struggle on frozen or prepared diets, including seahorses, pipefish, mandarin dragonets, larval clownfish, and other pelagic-spawn fry. A culture is typically run in a small 20–40 L vessel: bare-bottom for easy cleaning, gently aerated with a single airstone, with no powerheads (mysids are easily sucked into pumps and damaged by strong flow). Water is generally maintained at around 24–26°C and a specific gravity of roughly 1.015–1.023 (20–30 ppt), although the species tolerates a much wider salinity range in the wild. Some keepers run cultures at the lower end of this range to reduce salt costs. Photoperiod is commonly cited at 16 hours light and 8 hours dark. A stocking density of around 20 adults per litre is reported as optimal for sustained brood production.

Brood pouches develop within roughly 10–20 days of hatching; broods of around 5–7 young are released, and brood duration shortens noticeably with warmer water — roughly 15 days at 16°C down to 5 days at 29°C. Adults rarely live more than 2–3 months, so a culture operates as a rolling generation rather than a stable long-lived stock. Mysids are carnivorous and will cannibalise juveniles if underfed, so consistent feeding is essential — typically newly-hatched Artemia nauplii once or twice a day, supplemented with live phytoplankton or finely powdered dry food if available. Like most crustaceans, they are extremely sensitive to copper-based medications and to elevated ammonia or nitrite levels, so regular water changes and gentle mechanical filtration are part of the routine.
breedingNotes: Breeding is the entire point of keeping mysids — a healthy culture self-sustains through continuous brood release. Females develop a marsupium (brood pouch) at around 10–12 days post-hatch and produce successive broods of roughly 5–7 young over the following weeks. Maintaining a productive culture at scale tends to be demanding, as cannibalism and water-quality sensitivity are the main obstacles to consistent yields.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source carries Mysidopsis/Americamysis bahia only as a frozen food ingredient (multiple frozen mysis cube products) — there is no live culture product page. Husbandry parameters were therefore drawn from the cross-verification sources listed below; LiveAquaria is retained as primary per the assignment.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Americamysis bahia
additional.notes: Used as taxonomy and biology backbone (family Mysidae, order Mysida, native range, adult size 4.4–9.4 mm, brood biology, EPA test organism status). Reef2Reef Wiki (assigned secondary) returned HTTP 403 during this run — swapped to Wikipedia as the species-page secondary.

additional.name: MBL Aquaculture — Mysid Shrimp organism datasheet
additional.notes: Commercial mysid producer; confirms 4.4–9.4 mm adult length, estuarine habitat, salinity tolerance >15 ppt with peak abundance around 30 ppt, and use as live feed for seahorses, seadragons and pipefish.

additional.name: AquamarinePower — Mysis Shrimp culture guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-facing culture guide. Provides temperature 20–30°C, salinity 20–25 ppt, pH 8.0–8.3, 20–40 gallon culture tank size, Artemia nauplii feeding regime, and 5–10/L culture density for hobby setups. Cross-confirms sizing and brood biology.

additional.name: EPA — Culturing Americamysis bahia (training video supplement)
additional.notes: Authoritative laboratory culture protocol: 26°C target, 20–30 ppt salinity, 16h light / 8h dark photoperiod, ~20 adults/L stocking density, sexual maturity at 12–20 days, multiple broods per female. Used to anchor temperature and photoperiod choices.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Like all members of the malacostraca (the large crustacean group that includes shrimps, crabs, and lobsters), mysids moult repeatedly as they grow. In this species moults are very small and frequent — occurring every few days during the juvenile phase — and are generally not observed individually in a culture vessel.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Continuous brood release in a maintained culture. Females typically reach sexual maturity within 10–20 days and carry batches of around 5–7 young. Brood duration shortens with temperature (about 15 days at 16°C, 10 days at 20°C, 5 days at 29°C). Cannibalism of juveniles by adults is the main practical obstacle to sustained yields.
